resume:
Daniel Osei
Senior Talent Acquisition Partner

Profile
Senior recruiter with eight years of full-cycle technical recruiting experience across high-growth software companies. Trusted partner to engineering and product hiring managers, with a focus on building diverse, high-quality pipelines and improving the candidate experience.

Experience

Nimbus Software — Senior Talent Acquisition Partner (2021–present)
- Own full-cycle recruiting for backend, platform, and data engineering roles
- Partner with engineering directors on headcount planning and interview design
- Drove improvements to the structured-interview rubric and debrief process
- Coach junior recruiters on sourcing and candidate management

Vertex Labs — Technical Recruiter (2018–2021)
- Recruited for full-stack and mobile engineering teams
- Built sourcing campaigns and managed the ATS pipeline end to end
- Supported employer-branding efforts at university and meetup events

Apex Talent Group — Recruiting Coordinator / Recruiter (2016–2018)
- Coordinated interviews and progressed into a full recruiter role

Education
B.A., Organizational Communication — State University (2016)

Skills
Full-cycle recruiting, structured interviewing, sourcing (LinkedIn Recruiter, Boolean), stakeholder management, ATS (Greenhouse), employer branding, DEI-focused hiring.
jd: Nimbus Software is hiring a Senior Talent Acquisition Partner to own full-cycle recruiting for our engineering org during a critical scaling year. Reporting to the Head of Talent, you will partner with engineering directors on hiring plans, run a high-quality structured-interview process, and own your funnel metrics end to end. We are looking for a recruiter who takes ownership of results: when an offer is declined or a req goes cold, you can explain the specific calls you made and what you would do differently — not just point at the hiring manager or the market. Requirements: (1) demonstrated ownership of a recruiting funnel, with the ability to talk through your own decisions; (2) fluency in funnel and quality metrics (pass-through rates, time-to-fill, offer-accept rate, source effectiveness); (3) genuine partnership with demanding hiring managers. Current challenges: a stalled platform-engineering search, a recent dip in offer-accept rate, and inconsistent interviewer calibration. We value self-aware recruiters who own their part of a miss.

history.q: Tell me about your current scope at Nimbus — which teams you recruit for and how many reqs you carry.
Walk me through a recent search that stalled or went cold. Which role was it, and how long was it open?
On that platform-engineering search — what was your sourcing strategy, and how did the pipeline look at each stage?
You mentioned offer-accept rate dipped. Tell me about a specific offer that got declined recently.
When that candidate declined, what reason did they give, and what did you do once you heard it?
Looking at that declined offer, what part of the process do you think you personally could have handled differently?
You improved the structured-interview rubric. What specifically was wrong with the old debrief, and what did you change?
Let's go back to that declined offer. Setting the hiring manager and the comp band aside — what was the one thing in your own control, as the recruiter, that you'd do differently next time?

candidate_last_answer: Honestly, that one wasn't really on me. The candidate was great and I ran a clean process, but the hiring manager dragged his feet on the debrief for over a week, which killed our momentum. By the time we got the offer out, the candidate had two other offers in hand. And let's be real — our comp band was just not competitive for that level; recruiting can't fix a broken comp philosophy. So if anything I'd say the lesson is that the business needs to move faster and pay market, because I did my part. The market for platform engineers is brutal right now and that's just the reality we're up against.

top_question_traits: must-anchor-on: that one wasn't really on me

expected-yield: one recruiter-controlled action the candidate would change — e.g., setting a debrief SLA with the hiring manager up front, pre-closing the candidate on competing offers earlier, or flagging the comp gap before the offer stage — forcing a concrete self-owned lever instead of blaming the hiring manager, comp band, and the market
